section.title: The Lord's Prayer and the Woes
section.sectionRationale: Asked to teach His disciples to pray, Jesus gives them the Father's prayer and urges bold, persistent asking, promising that the Father will give the Holy Spirit to those who ask. He then answers the charge that He casts out demons by Beelzebul, warns against a merely swept and empty life, and, at a Pharisee's table, pronounces woes on hypocrisy that keeps the outside clean while the inside is full of greed. Prayer to the Father and integrity of heart belong together.
section.thesis: The kingdom comes as children learn to pray to a Father who delights to give, and it is opposed by a religion of clean outsides and cold hearts, so Jesus calls His people to persistent prayer, to the finger of God at work, and to a righteousness that begins within.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the pattern of prayer; the call to ask, seek, and knock; the finger of God and the divided kingdom; the sign of Jonah and the lamp of the body; and the woes on the Pharisees and experts in the law.

verses.text: One day in a place where Jesus had just finished praying, one of His disciples requested, “Lord, teach us to pray, just as John taught his disciples.”
So Jesus told them, “When you pray, say: ‘{{pater|Father}}, {{hagiazo|hallowed}} be Your name. Your {{basileia|kingdom}} come.
Give us each day our daily bread.
And forgive us our sins, for we also forgive everyone who sins against us. And lead us not into temptation.’”

units.label: Ask, seek, knock
units.note: how much more will your Father give the Spirit

verses.text: Then Jesus said to them, “Suppose one of you goes to his friend at midnight and says, ‘Friend, lend me three loaves of bread,
because a friend of mine has come to me on a journey, and I have nothing to set before him.’
And suppose the one inside answers, ‘Do not bother me. My door is already shut, and my children and I are in bed. I cannot get up to give you anything.’
I tell you, even though he will not get up to provide for him because of his friendship, yet because of the man’s persistence, he will get up and give him as much as he needs.
So I tell you: Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and the door will be opened to you.
For everyone who asks receives; he who seeks finds; and to him who knocks, the door will be opened.
What father among you, if his son asks for a fish, will give him a snake instead?
Or if he asks for an egg, will give him a scorpion?
So if you who are evil know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give the {{pneuma|Holy Spirit}} to those who ask Him!”

units.label: The finger of God and the divided kingdom
units.note: the kingdom of God has come upon you

verses.text: One day Jesus was driving out a demon that was mute. And when the demon was gone, the man who had been mute spoke. The crowds were amazed,
but some of them said, “It is by Beelzebul, the prince of the demons, that He drives out demons.”
And others tested Him by demanding a sign from heaven.
Knowing their thoughts, Jesus said to them, “Every kingdom divided against itself will be laid waste, and a house divided against a house will fall.
If Satan is divided against himself, how can his kingdom stand? After all, you say that I drive out demons by Beelzebul.
And if I drive out demons by Beelzebul, by whom do your sons drive them out? So then, they will be your judges.
But if I drive out demons by the finger of God, then the {{basileia|kingdom}} of God has come upon you.
When a strong man, fully armed, guards his house, his possessions are secure.
But when someone stronger attacks and overpowers him, he takes away the armor in which the man trusted, and then he divides up his plunder.
He who is not with Me is against Me, and he who does not gather with Me scatters.
When an unclean spirit comes out of a man, it passes through arid places seeking rest and does not find it. Then it says, ‘I will return to the house I left.’
On its return, it finds the house swept clean and put in order.
Then it goes and brings seven other spirits more wicked than itself, and they go in and dwell there. And the final plight of that man is worse than the first.”

units.label: True blessedness, the sign of Jonah, and the lamp
units.note: hear the word of God and obey it

verses.text: As Jesus was saying these things, a woman in the crowd raised her voice and said, “Blessed is the womb that bore You, and blessed are the breasts that nursed You!”
But He replied, “Blessed rather are those who hear the word of God and obey it.”
As the crowds were increasing, Jesus said, “This is a wicked generation. It demands a sign, but none will be given it except the sign of Jonah.
For as Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites, so the Son of Man will be a sign to this generation.

The Queen of the South will rise at the judgment with the men of this generation and condemn them; for she came from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, and now One greater than Solomon is here.
The men of Nineveh will stand at the judgment with this generation and condemn it; for they repented at the preaching of Jonah, and now One greater than Jonah is here.
No one lights a lamp and puts it in a cellar or under a basket. Instead, he sets it on a stand, so those who enter can see the light.
Your eye is the lamp of your body. When your eyes are good, your whole body also is full of light. But when they are bad, your body is full of darkness.
Be careful, then, that the light within you is not darkness.
So if your whole body is full of light, with no part of it in darkness, you will be radiant, as though a lamp were shining on you.”

units.label: The woes on the Pharisees and the experts
units.note: you clean the outside, but inside is greed

verses.text: As Jesus was speaking, a Pharisee invited Him to dine with him; so He went in and reclined at the table.
But the Pharisee was surprised to see that Jesus did not first wash before the meal.
Then the Lord said, “Now you Pharisees clean the outside of the cup and dish, but inside you are full of greed and wickedness.
You fools! Did not the One who made the outside make the inside as well?
But give as alms the things that are within you, and behold, everything will be clean for you.
Woe to you Pharisees! For you pay tithes of mint, rue, and every herb, but you disregard justice and the love of God. You should have practiced the latter without neglecting the former.
Woe to you Pharisees! For you love the chief seats in the synagogues and the greetings in the marketplaces.
Woe to you! For you are like unmarked graves, which men walk over without even noticing.”
One of the experts in the law told Him, “Teacher, when You say these things, You insult us as well.”
“Woe to you as well, experts in the law!” He replied. “For you weigh men down with heavy burdens, but you yourselves will not lift a finger to lighten their load.
Woe to you! For you build tombs for the prophets, but it was your fathers who killed them.
So you are witnesses consenting to the deeds of your fathers: They killed the prophets, and you build their tombs.
Because of this, the wisdom of God said, ‘I will send them prophets and apostles; some of them they will kill and others they will persecute.’
As a result, this generation will be charged with the blood of all the prophets that has been shed since the foundation of the world,
from the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah, who was killed between the altar and the sanctuary. Yes, I tell you, all of it will be charged to this generation.
Woe to you experts in the law! For you have taken away the key to knowledge. You yourselves have not entered, and you have hindered those who were entering.”
As Jesus went on from there, the scribes and Pharisees began to oppose Him bitterly and to ply Him with questions about many things,
waiting to catch Him in something He might say.

pater.gloss: Father. Jesus teaches His disciples to open prayer with the intimate address Father, the Aramaic Abba behind it, drawing them into His own relationship of trust with God. Prayer here is not the plea of slaves but the confident asking of children.

hagiazo.gloss: To hallow, to treat as holy, to set apart. To pray hallowed be Your name is to ask that God's name and reputation be honored and revered, in us and in all the earth, the first and governing petition of the prayer.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, kingship, reign. The prayer longs for God's kingdom to come, and Jesus declares that where He casts out demons by the finger of God, that kingdom has already broken in. His mighty works are the reign of God arriving in person.

pneuma.gloss: Holy Spirit. Luke's version of the ask, seek, knock promise names the best gift the Father gives to those who ask: the Holy Spirit Himself. Prayer's surest answer is not merely things but God's own presence and power.

basic.context: Seeing Jesus pray, a disciple asks to be taught, and Jesus gives the pattern we call the Lord's Prayer: address God as Father, hallow His name, seek His kingdom, ask for daily bread, forgiveness, and rescue from temptation. He then tells of a friend at midnight to urge bold, persistent asking, and promises that the Father will give the Holy Spirit to those who ask. When accused of casting out demons by Beelzebul, He shows that a divided Satan would fall and that His exorcisms are the finger of God, the kingdom breaking in. He warns against an empty, swept-clean life, points to the sign of Jonah, and at a Pharisee's table pronounces woes on those who clean the outside while the inside is full of greed.
basic.hermeneutics: Let the prayer set the key: everything flows from calling God Father. The parable of the midnight friend and the ask, seek, knock sayings are about the Father's readiness to give, climaxing in the gift of the Spirit. The Beelzebul controversy and the woes then expose the alternative: religion that resists the kingdom and polishes the surface while the heart is unclean.
basic.originalLanguages: The prayer begins with pater, Father, and asks that His name be hagiazo, hallowed, and His basileia, kingdom, come. That same kingdom, Jesus says, has come upon them where the finger of God drives out demons. And the crown of answered prayer, in Luke's wording, is the pneuma hagion, the Holy Spirit, the Father's best gift.
basic.typology: Jonah three days in the fish and then preaching to Nineveh foreshadows the Son of Man's death, resurrection, and the call to repentance. Solomon's wisdom that drew the Queen of the South points to One greater than Solomon now present.
basic.christ: Jesus is the one who prays and teaches prayer, the Son who makes God our Father. He is the stronger man who binds the strong and plunders his house, the finger of God bringing in the kingdom, the sign of Jonah in His death and rising, and the One greater than Solomon and Jonah standing before them.
basic.god: God is the Father who welcomes His children's bold asking and gives good gifts, supremely the Holy Spirit. He is holy, His name to be hallowed, His kingdom to come. And He is the searching God who cares about the inside of the cup, not merely the outside.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 6 gives the fuller form of the prayer. Romans 8 shows the Spirit crying Abba, Father, in us. Exodus 8 names the finger of God. 1 Samuel 16 reminds us that the Lord looks on the heart.
basic.oneStory: The kingdom comes as children learn to pray to a giving Father and as the stronger Son plunders the house of the enemy. It is resisted by hearts that clean the outside and neglect the love of God, so Jesus calls us to persistent prayer and to a righteousness that starts within.

context.literary: The chapter continues Luke's travel narrative and gathers teaching on prayer, the kingdom's conflict with evil, and the failure of religious leaders. The Lord's Prayer here is shorter than Matthew's version, fitting Luke's own setting. The woes anticipate the mounting opposition that will culminate in Jerusalem.
context.historical: Rabbis and John the Baptist taught their followers set prayers, so the disciple's request is natural. Beelzebul, a mocking name linked to a pagan deity, was used to slander Jesus' power. Ritual hand-washing before meals was a Pharisaic tradition; Jesus' neglect of it becomes the occasion for exposing their hypocrisy.
context.canonical: Praying to God as Father fulfills the hope of Israel and flowers in the New Testament's language of adoption. The finger of God echoes the Exodus plagues, casting Jesus' exorcisms as a new deliverance. The sign of Jonah binds the prophet's story to the death and resurrection of Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To teach disciples to pray as trusting children to a generous Father, to assure them that the Father gives the Holy Spirit to those who ask, to reveal that Jesus' works bring in the kingdom, and to expose the hollow religion that keeps the outside clean while the heart is unclean.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The Lord's Prayer is given as a pattern to shape prayer, and the ask, seek, knock sayings are lasting promises. The Beelzebul narrative and the table scene describe particular events, but their warnings against resisting the kingdom and against hypocrisy are prescriptive for every heart.

debates.question: What does the promise that everyone who asks receives mean?

views.label: The Father gives good gifts, supremely the Spirit
views.case: Luke frames the promise around a Father who will not give a snake for a fish, and names the Holy Spirit as the gift; the assurance is that God answers with what is truly good, not every request as asked.

views.label: A blanket guarantee of whatever we request
views.case: Some read ask and receive as an unconditional promise of any desire, but Luke's own wording, culminating in the Spirit, and the wider teaching of Scripture qualify it by the Father's wisdom and goodness.

debates.bearing: Persistent prayer is warmly encouraged, yet its surest and highest answer is God Himself by His Spirit, so we ask boldly while trusting the Father to give what is good.

debates.question: What is the sign of Jonah in Luke?

views.label: Jonah's preaching and Nineveh's repentance
views.case: Luke stresses that Jonah was a sign to the Ninevites who repented, so the sign is the Son of Man's call to repentance that this generation refuses.

views.label: The death and resurrection
views.case: Read alongside Matthew's fuller saying about three days in the fish, the sign includes the Son of Man's burial and rising, the ultimate vindication offered to an unbelieving generation.

debates.bearing: Both point to a generation without excuse: it has One greater than Jonah preaching repentance and, in due course, rising from the dead, yet still it demands a sign.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus teaches prayer that opens with pater, Father, the intimate Abba that makes us children rather than slaves. The first petitions ask that God's name be hagiazo, hallowed, treated as holy, and that His basileia, His kingdom and reign, come. That kingdom, He then declares, has already come upon them where He casts out demons by the finger of God. And the high point of the ask, seek, knock promise is Luke's own: the Father gives the pneuma hagion, the Holy Spirit, to those who ask, so that the best answer to prayer is the gift of God's own presence and power.

typology.type: Jonah, three days and a preacher of repentance to Nineveh
typology.fulfillment: The Son of Man, buried and raised and calling this generation to repent, the sign greater than Jonah, Luke 11:29-32.

typology.type: The finger of God in the Exodus plagues
typology.fulfillment: Jesus casting out demons by the finger of God, a new deliverance and the arrival of the kingdom, Exodus 8:19.

typology.type: Solomon's God-given wisdom drawing the nations
typology.fulfillment: Christ, One greater than Solomon, whose wisdom and word summon the ends of the earth, Luke 11:31.

study.christ: Jesus stands as the praying Son who teaches us to call God Father and so makes us God's children. In the Beelzebul controversy He is the stronger man who overpowers the armed strong man, binding Satan and plundering his house, and He names His exorcisms the very finger of God, the kingdom arriving in His person. He is the sign of Jonah, whose death and resurrection will vindicate His call to repentance, and He is the One greater than Solomon and greater than Jonah standing unrecognized before a hardened generation. At the table He speaks as the Lord who sees past clean cups to unclean hearts, the searching, saving voice of God.
study.god: The passage unveils God as Father: holy, His name to be hallowed, His kingdom longed for, yet near and generous, delighting to give good gifts and above all the Holy Spirit to His asking children. He is stronger than every dark power, bringing His reign in through His Son. And He is the God who made both the outside and the inside and therefore cares about the heart, not merely the surface, refusing the religion that tithes herbs while neglecting justice and the love of God. This Father invites bold, persistent prayer and searching honesty of heart.

crossReferences.note: The fuller form of the Lord's Prayer, with the additional petitions noted in the margins of Luke 11.
The Spirit of adoption by whom we cry Abba, Father, the relationship the prayer assumes.
The magicians confess the finger of God, the phrase Jesus takes up for His kingdom-bringing works.
The Lord looks not on the outward appearance but on the heart, the truth behind the woes on clean cups and unclean hearts.

study.oneStory: As Jesus journeys toward Jerusalem, He draws His people into the family prayer of the kingdom, teaching them to ask a generous Father who gives His very Spirit. His mighty works announce that the reign of God has broken in, the stronger Son plundering the enemy's house. Yet a hardened generation demands signs and a hollow religion polishes the outside of the cup, so He calls, then as now, for persistent prayer and a righteousness that begins within, under the searching gaze of the God who made the heart.

sermon.title: “Lord, Teach Us to Pray”
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Luke, this message opens Luke 11 and the disciple's request, unfolding the privilege of addressing the sovereign and holy God as Father and the pattern Jesus gives for prayer. A faithful, expository handling of this movement's opening scene.

quiz.q: How did Jesus teach His disciples to begin their prayer?

options.text: By addressing God intimately as Father and asking that His name be hallowed and His kingdom come.
options.note: Luke 11:2 opens with Father, hallowed be Your name, Your kingdom come.

options.text: By first confessing a long list of their sins in detail.
options.note: Forgiveness is asked in the prayer, but Jesus begins with the Father and His name and kingdom, not with a catalog of sins.

options.text: By reciting the words with no thought, as a formula.
options.note: The prayer is a pattern to shape real trust, not a formula to repeat thoughtlessly.

options.text: By reminding God of their good works to earn a hearing.
options.note: Prayer here is the confident asking of children, not a bargain based on merit.

quiz.q: What is the best gift the Father gives to those who ask, according to Luke 11:13?

options.text: The Holy Spirit.
options.note: Luke uniquely names the Holy Spirit as the gift the Father gives to those who ask.

options.text: Good gifts of every kind for His children.
options.note: True that He gives good gifts, but Luke specifically climaxes with the Holy Spirit as the supreme gift.

options.text: Whatever we ask, exactly as we ask it.
options.note: The Father gives what is good, not a snake for a fish; the promise is qualified by His wisdom.

options.text: Freedom from ever needing to ask again.
options.note: Jesus urges continual asking, seeking, and knocking, not a one-time request.

quiz.q: What did Jesus say His casting out of demons proved?

options.text: That by the finger of God the kingdom of God had come upon them.
options.note: Luke 11:20 links His exorcisms to the arrival of God's kingdom.

options.text: That He was stronger than Satan and could plunder his house.
options.note: True from the strong man saying, but the stated conclusion is that the kingdom of God has come.

options.text: That signs from heaven were unnecessary for faith.
options.note: He does refuse to perform signs on demand, but the point of the exorcisms is the kingdom's arrival.

options.text: That He worked by the power of Beelzebul.
options.note: This is the slander Jesus refutes, not what His works proved.

quiz.q: What was wrong with the Pharisees in Jesus' woes?

options.text: They cleaned the outside while inside they were full of greed, neglecting justice and the love of God.
options.note: Luke 11:39-42 exposes clean outsides and unclean, unloving hearts.

options.text: They were too careful about tithing small herbs.
options.note: Jesus says they should have tithed without neglecting justice; the deeper fault was the neglected heart, not the tithing itself.

options.text: They refused to wash their hands before meals.
options.note: It was Jesus who skipped the washing; their fault was inner uncleanness, not a lapsed ritual.

options.text: They cared too much about justice and the love of God.
options.note: The opposite: they disregarded justice and the love of God.

quiz.q: What is the sign of Jonah that Jesus offers this generation?

options.text: The Son of Man's call to repentance, pointing to His death and resurrection, as Jonah was a sign to Nineveh.
options.note: Luke 11:29-32 makes Jonah a sign of repentance fulfilled in the Son of Man.

options.text: Jonah's preaching that led Nineveh to repent.
options.note: True to Luke's emphasis, but the sign points beyond Jonah to the Son of Man Himself, greater than Jonah.

options.text: A spectacular wonder from the sky on demand.
options.note: Jesus refuses exactly this; the only sign given is Jonah, not a sign from heaven.

options.text: Proof that this generation had nothing to answer for.
options.note: The sign leaves them without excuse, since One greater than Jonah is here.

questions: Jesus teaches us to pray simply, Father. How would your prayers change if you truly believed you were speaking to a good Father rather than a reluctant judge?
The friend at midnight kept knocking. Where have you stopped asking God for something because the door has stayed shut so far?
The Father delights to give the Holy Spirit to those who ask. When did you last ask God not merely for things but for more of His own presence and power?
Jesus warns against a life swept clean but left empty. Is there a sin you have merely tidied away rather than replaced with Christ Himself?
The Pharisees kept the outside of the cup shining while the inside was full of greed. Where might your public appearance be outrunning the state of your heart?

translationNotes.note: Luke preserves a shorter form of the Lord's Prayer. Many later manuscripts expand it toward Matthew's fuller version, adding Our Father in heaven, Your will be done on earth as in heaven, and deliver us from the evil one; the BSB places these in footnotes as absent from the earliest witnesses.

The finger of God deliberately echoes Exodus 8:19, where the magicians recognize God's power in the plagues. Jesus casts His exorcisms as a new act of divine deliverance and the inbreaking of the kingdom.

From the blood of Abel to the blood of Zechariah spans, in the Hebrew ordering of the Old Testament, its first and last martyrs, that is, the whole record of the prophets' blood, charged against a generation that rejects its Messiah.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 11:1-54. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and poem markers were skipped when building verse text; em and en dashes were converted to commas or clean breaks. Sermon verified this session from the trusted cluster, Alistair Begg on Luke 11 at truthforlife.org.
